meta.ownerBD: Michael Scarpellini, Head of Partnerships (enterprise / out of self-serve scope)
meta.northStar: Liquid revenue via self-serve. First paying customers plus a repeatable acquisition channel with stable CAC.
meta.sourceNote: Source of truth: the marketing strategy spine and the Minimum Viable Context. Curated strategy is human-edited; experiments and campaign metrics refresh automatically each week.

overview.elevator: Self-serve AI on-model imagery for fashion brands. Create an AI model, paste a product URL, get campaign-ready 2K imagery in minutes. It replaces the photoshoot, not one incumbent tool.
overview.what: Ablo Studio is the on-model imagery layer for a fashion brand's drop. Three steps: create your model, import your catalog, dress and shoot. The defensible job is the believable, editorial-quality on-model shot of a body the brand cannot easily or cheaply photograph: babies, plus and diverse bodies, swim fit.

howItWorks.title: Create your model
howItWorks.body: Choose ethnicity, hair, body type, style, or upload a reference photo to keep real facial features. One editorial model, 12 poses. 5 credits (10 with a reference photo).

howItWorks.title: Import your catalog
howItWorks.body: Paste a product URL from Shopify, Cafe24, or most platforms. Product images are extracted automatically. Tops, dresses, pants, outerwear, shoes, accessories. Import is free.

howItWorks.title: Dress and shoot
howItWorks.body: Generate the try-on at 2K, multiple poses, custom backgrounds, plus AI edits to lighting, shadows, and scene by text prompt. Try-on 5 credits, edit 2 credits.

posture.v: Ablo.ai is the umbrella AI implementation company. Ablo Studio is one of its own products and the current focus. This OS covers Studio as a self-serve product. The enterprise / custom-build motion runs separately, owned by Michael.

Tech-led, finding its market. The team had the AI capability and built the most obvious productized shape. Positioning, ICP, and pricing are still in motion and will sharpen as the market signal does.

Free tools (PhotoRoom, Pimeli) already own background removal and flat lays for about $5/mo. Studio must not wedge there. Every message leads from the on-model shot of bodies you cannot cheaply shoot.

currentFocus: Fix the signup-modal drop. Only 46 of 127 opens finish, and the email magic-link path leaks almost everyone (52 requested, 3 logged in). Promote Google sign-in, demote email. The single highest-leverage fix on the live funnel.

Close the try-on activation gap. 59 entered the studio, 28 generated a model (53% never do). Find the broken or slow control before the first generate. Free-tier credit rebalance shipped to give new users two full generations.
Run the price-ask test. Manually onboard 5 kids founders and 5 swim / size-inclusive founders, watch them hit try-on, then ask what they would pay. Free signups tell us almost nothing about revenue.
Find one repeatable acquisition channel with stable CAC. The autopilot plus a $200/week validation budget is the test rig.

goals.northStar: Liquid revenue via self-serve: first paying customers plus a repeatable acquisition channel.
goals.theBrag: First 5 paying self-serve customers at a validated CAC under $300 and ARPU at or above $50/mo, within roughly 6 to 8 weeks. This converts the ICP from hypothesis to proven.

kpoGoals.text: Signup to paid at or above 8%, or learn the real number and reset the model.
Fix the signup-modal drop and the try-on activation gap. Product-owned, but it gates every paid dollar. This is the #1 blocker.
Run the price-ask test (5 kids founders + 5 swim / size-inclusive) to get real ARPU and willingness-to-pay, then promote a segment to Core.
Identify one repeatable acquisition channel with stable CAC. The autopilot and the validation budget are the test rig.

revenueLevers.name: Grow top-of-funnel with the core audience
revenueLevers.body: More qualified signups from the Kids and Swim / size-inclusive wedge via a repeatable paid channel. We have an ICP and creative that resonate; we lack volume and a stable-CAC engine.

revenueLevers.name: Increase conversion: signup to activation to paid
revenueLevers.body: Fix the signup-modal drop and the try-on activation gap. This moves CAC more than any budget change, and it costs nothing in spend. Do 1 and 2 together: 1 without 2 burns money; 2 without 1 has nothing to convert.

revenueLevers.name: Increase customer value (ARPU)
revenueLevers.body: Credit top-ups and a power-user tier so heavy-drop brands expand past the $50 base. Required for the unit economics to clear.

revenueLevers.name: Efficiency
revenueLevers.body: Concentrate budget on converting segments. Already done: about 90% on Kids and women's.

unitEconomics.sub: $50 base + ~$30 expansion (top-ups / power tier). Annual ~$960. Flat $50 with no expansion makes the economics tight, so push top-ups.
at ~$20 CPL to hold CAC at or below $250.

goals.arc: Wave 1 (done) → Wave 2 LinkedIn replication (done) → customer interviews shape Wave 3 messaging → end-of-June goal: first paying customer. At $200/week this is a validation budget, not a growth budget. The lever that decides viability here is conversion and ARPU, not spend.

okrs.objective: Turn cheap signups into the first proof that the wedge produces revenue.

krs.kr: Fix the two funnel leaks (signup modal + first generate)
Price-ask test with 5 Kids + 5 Swim founders

okrs.objective: Find one channel that reliably turns spend into paying customers, then scale it.

krs.kr: Paying self-serve customers at validated CAC < $300
One acquisition channel with stable CAC, identified and scaled
ARPU with a top-up / power tier live

icp.buyer: Founder / Owner / CEO of a small (2 to 50, mostly 2 to 10 employee) US fashion brand in Retail Apparel & Fashion or Apparel Manufacturing.
icp.wedgeNote: One buyer, several category wedges. The segments differ by product category, which is what changes the creative, the demo, and the pain. Nothing is Core yet: Studio is pre-revenue, so maturity reflects engagement and activation evidence, not revenue. The price-ask test is what promotes a segment to Core.
icp.geo: US-first and decisively. LinkedIn US CTR 12.2% vs 3.9 to 5.5% in the EU. EU is a secondary test, not a current priority.

tiers.name: Kids & babywear
tiers.company: Small US kids & babywear DTC brand
tiers.why: You cannot cheaply or repeatedly shoot babies and toddlers: expensive, unreliable, age out of the product in weeks. The hard part is the model, exactly where Studio is defensible. Best Meta funnel, cheapest signups, an activated real brand (Agenzia Kids).

tiers.name: Swim & size-inclusive
tiers.company: Small US swimwear & size-inclusive women's brand
tiers.why: The most expensive, most logistically painful shoot in fashion, and again the hard part is the model: many bodies, real fit on diverse and plus bodies, heavy SKU counts. Best Meta CTR (swim 2.97%), an activated real brand (Water Vixen Swim).

tiers.name: Contemporary women's
tiers.company: Small US independent / contemporary women's brand
tiers.why: The broad wedge inside the firmographic ICP. General fashion signal.

tiers.name: Menswear
tiers.why: Weakest Meta pull: lowest CTR (1.91%), fewest signups. Demote unless a sharper sub-need (fast collab / co-branded mock-ups) tests differently. (The ad set labeled "streetwear & menswear" was the men's campaign, not streetwear.)

tiers.name: Agencies / photo studios
tiers.why: B2B2B: one studio serves many brands. Showed up on LinkedIn at lower intent. A different motion.

tiers.name: Enterprise / IP licensors
tiers.why: The enterprise prize. A named-account BD motion (Terranova, Warner Bros, Hasbro), owned by Michael, anchored on IP-clean output. Not an ad-targetable segment.

rubric.name: Unsolvable problem
rubric.body: What can they not do cheaply otherwise? If a free tool or a $500 photographer already solves it, there is no wedge.

rubric.name: Recurrence
rubric.body: One-time need is one-time revenue. Every-drop need is a subscription.

rubric.name: Willingness + ability to pay
rubric.body: Above the free-tool floor of about $5/mo.

rubric.name: Acquirability (CAC)
rubric.body: Reachable repeatably and cheaply. A hard requirement, not a nice-to-have, because the runway is short.

icp.lesson: The PhotoRoom signal is the most important thing the data taught us. A streetwear founder we interviewed (Marco) churned to PhotoRoom (~$5/mo), which already did everything he needed: background removal, flat lays, ghost mannequin. That is the commoditized part of the workflow. Studio's only defensible job is generating a believable on-model image of bodies a brand cannot easily or cheaply shoot. That single reframing reorders every segment: it rewards Kids and Swim / size-inclusive (the hard problem is the model) and punishes Menswear (its loud need is the commoditized part).
icp.decisiveTest: Both moves end in a price ask, because free signups tell us almost nothing. Manually onboard 5 kids founders (start with Agenzia Kids) and 4 to 5 swim / plus brands (start with Water Vixen Swim). Watch them hit try-on, ask the four discovery questions and "would you pay, how much." Decision rule: whichever segment converts a card first, at a price that clears CAC and sits clearly above the ~$5 free-tool floor, is ICP #1.
icp.realSignups: Who actually showed up: micro independent brands (ALYYO, Naked Slang, Zero Core, NeevaD London), a swimwear brand (Water Vixen Swim, activated twice), a kids label (Agenzia Kids), a couture bridal brand (BENI), a craft brand (ElilouCrea), and a freelance fashion photographer. Geography US-heavy, then Italy, Spain, UK, France.

positioning.statement: Studio replaces the fashion photoshoot and the cobbled-together free-AI stack, not one incumbent tool. The buyer's old way is an expensive shoot ($5K to $50K, weeks) or gluing together free and horizontal AI (PhotoRoom, Pimeli, Midjourney). Secondary frame: a Vertical Solution vs horizontal image AI.
positioning.differentiator: Within "AI imagery", free tools already own background removal and flat lays. The defensible job is the believable, editorial-quality on-model shot of a body the brand cannot easily or cheaply photograph: babies, plus and diverse bodies, swim fit. Every message leads from that.

quad.today: Founders / owners of small US fashion brands (kids, swim, size-inclusive) refreshing imagery every drop.
quad.year: + in-house marketers at growing DTC fashion brands; + EU.

quad.today: Self-serve AI on-model imagery built for fashion: create models, import your catalog, dress and shoot.
quad.year: The on-model imagery layer for any fashion brand's drop workflow.

quad.today: The photoshoot ($5K to $50K, weeks) + the cobbled free / horizontal-AI stack (PhotoRoom, Pimeli, Midjourney).
quad.year: + vertical AI try-on incumbents (Raspberry, Botika) as the category matures.

quad.today: Editorial quality (the stitching, the skin, the light), fashion-native, including the bodies you cannot easily shoot, in minutes. Your drop, your story, your way.
quad.year: + brand-guidelines-as-context (your model, your aesthetic, every drop) + IP-clean output.

proofToday: The output itself: the imagery is the strongest proof.
proofToEarn: A public, named Studio customer with permission to be quoted.
A named reference SMB story (a kids or swim brand using Studio in production).
Aggregate stats: shoots replaced, hours and cost saved.
positioning.parentCredibility: Ablo has worked with Crocs and Balmain-class brands at the parent level. This opens enterprise doors and lends trust, but those brands have not used Studio. It is not Studio proof and cannot be cited as self-serve proof.

perceptions.text: Ablo Studio gives me campaign-ready, on-model photos of my products in minutes, including shots I could never get myself (my kids' line, every body type, swim fit).
AI imagery is now good enough that a small fashion brand can look like a big one. The photoshoot is becoming optional.
I can ship a drop's imagery on schedule without a photographer, a studio, or model casting.
It keeps my garments, fit, and brand intact. It is not AI slop.
This is built by people who actually work with fashion brands, not a generic image-AI toy.

messaging.perceptionsNote: Beliefs we want the audience to repeat back. Most content should ladder to #1 and #2.

taglines.line: No photographers. No studios. No hassle.

verbatim: Create your models. Import your catalog. Dress and shoot.
Look at the stitching. The skin. The light. Editorial quality.
Your drop. Your story. Your way.

homepageArchitecture.v: "no more boring shoots" + "Generate AI models, dress them in your products, and create campaign-ready imagery in minutes."
Create Model · Import Any Product · Campaign-Ready Results
Ethnicity · Hair Style · Body Type · 12 Poses · 2K Resolution · AI Edit · Download

gaps: Why Studio exists: no founding-thesis copy on the site.
Who it is for: "fashion brands" is too generic; the wedge needs surface area.
Proof: no customer logos, case studies, or aggregate stats yet.
Comparison clarity: no side-by-side against the photoshoot budget or generic AI.

voice.direction: Editorial, calm, premium, proof-forward. Keep the editorial design language, add explanatory copy and proof on pricing and conversion surfaces. Lead with the result, not the process.

rules.detail: Use commas, periods, parentheses, or restructure.
ICP, DTC, OKRs, CAC stay as acronyms.

rules.rule: Lead with the result, not the process.
rules.detail: The campaign-ready image first, the steps second.

rules.rule: When in doubt, cut a sentence.

rules.detail: No revolutionary, game-changing, unleash, or exclamation points.

twoSurfaces.guidance: Editorial, sparse, photo-led, confident. Let the image carry it.
Clearer, more explanatory, proof-forward. Still designed and quiet, but with more words.

doDont.dont: Unleash game-changing visuals at the speed of thought.

doDont.do: Look at the stitching. The skin. The light.
doDont.dont: Our cutting-edge AI delivers stunning, photorealistic results.

doDont.do: Every body type without booking 8 models.
doDont.dont: Diversity and inclusion, powered by next-gen AI.

voice.agentPrompt: You are writing for Ablo Studio, self-serve AI on-model imagery for fashion brands. Voice: editorial, calm, premium, proof-forward. Think SSENSE, Off-White, Linear, Arc'teryx. Lead with the result, not the process. Sparse beats complete; when in doubt, cut a sentence. Hard rules: never use em dashes (use commas, periods, parentheses, or restructure); keep acronyms as acronyms (ICP, DTC, CAC); no exclamation points; no AI hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, unleash); no generic-SaaS or startup-bro tone. Lead every message from the one wedge: campaign-ready, on-model photos of bodies a brand cannot easily or cheaply shoot (babies, plus and diverse bodies, swim fit) in minutes, not background removal (free tools own that). Audience: founder / owner of a small US fashion brand (kids, swim, size-inclusive). Proof we can use today: the output itself, the launch film, 50 free credits no card. Do not claim Crocs or Balmain as Studio customers (parent-level only). Do not write to the old $1,250 price.

experimentsCurated.intro: Every experiment carries a stable ID. Live PostHog A/B tests and tracked before/after ships refresh on each run, alongside the standing backlog.

liveFallback.name: Studio onboarding coachmarks
liveFallback.hypothesis: Tests whether showing the first-run coachmarks (the guided tips on the create-model screen: describe, refine selectors, generate, ready-made model) increases the share of users who generate a model. control = no coachmarks; test = coachmarks shown. 50/50 split, 100% rollout. Exposure fires when a user reaches the create-model step.
liveFallback.signal: Collecting data. Exposures are low (single digits) while paid delivery is paused, so results are not yet conclusive.

backlog.title: Try-on activation: unblock the first generate
backlog.hyp: There is a control on /studio that looks clickable but does not respond or is too slow before the first "Generate Model". Fixing it lifts the 53% who enter the studio but never generate.

backlog.title: Free-tier credit rebalance
backlog.hyp: Photo upload dropped 36 to 25 credits so a new user can complete 2 full generations with a custom model before credits run out. Closes the exhaust-credits-before-activation loop.

backlog.title: Brand guidelines / logo as context
backlog.hyp: Letting a brand upload its guidelines or logo as context is the highest-leverage product investment across all segments, because brand-integrity is the quality test in every segment.

signupExperiment.name: Signup modal: Google primary
signupExperiment.hypothesis: Promoting Continue with Google to the primary action and dropping email/password (magic link kept as a secondary link) lifts signup-modal completion. The magic-link path forced an inbox round-trip most people never returned from. Shipped May 27 (Google added), May 28 (email/password removed).
signupExperiment.signal: Measuring before vs after May 27. Live before/after completion computes on each refresh.

experimentsCurated.policy: Focus beats volume. Run no more than 2 to 3 experiments at once, one primary test per funnel stage. With traffic this low, more concurrent tests dilute the signal and slow every conclusion. Ship the winner, then start the next.

campaignsCurated.name: Signup Launch (2026-05)
structure: Wave 1: LinkedIn founder posts (Deniz 5/19, Won 5/20) + Thought Leader Ads (~$300), plus Meta paid across 4 segments.
Wave 2: Won-authored LinkedIn post (5/26), a replication test of Wave 1, not amplification.
Meta segments: kids (/toddler), curve / plus-size (/plus-size), womens-swim (/), menswear (/).
learnings: Kids is the cheapest, healthiest acquisition. Swim has the best attention. Menswear is the weakest paid pull.
Funnel leaks: the signup-modal drop, plus swim and menswear leaked at the landing page.
Tracking caveat: the /toddler and /plus-size pixels under-fired, so trust CTR and raw signups over cross-segment signup rate.
The Meta ads are run by the ablo-ads-autopilot skill on a 6-hour cron that kills losers and doubles down on winners. Product-agnostic and pluggable into any future launch.
lifecycle: Channel weighting is Meta-primary (about 80/20 vs LinkedIn) on current performance.
Lead routing: Alejo owns all inbound consumer and high-intent leads; Michael owns outbound B2B to a named US + Italy company list.
Klaviyo is the email tool (basic plan). Abandoned-cart email for the try-on stage is queued.

content.status: Deliberately a stub. The 90-day plan was BD-led and narrow: no social audience building, no founder content engine, no blog. A full content strategy may be premature by design. This holds the plan for when content goes in scope.
content.engine: The product's own output is the strongest content: before/after, "shot in minutes," per-segment model demos. Every generation is shareable proof, which makes content infinite, cheap, and credible.
content.recipe: The repeatable engine: with the MVC, the per-segment profiles, and the voice rules in this OS, an AI can draft on-brand ads, posts, and emails from a one-line brief. Awareness hook (the pain) → consideration (the bodies-you-cannot-shoot wedge + proof) → conversion (free 50 credits, the activation moment).
channels: Meta / IG works for Kids and Swim.
LinkedIn founder posts were the Wave 1 / 2 vehicle.
The decision still open: which one channel + audience to pour budget into.
toFill: Goal: what is content for (demand gen for the self-serve wedge, proof for enterprise, SEO, founder-led distribution).
Audience x stage: the message at each funnel stage, per lead segment.
Formats + cadence: what we can actually produce.
Document the prompt / recipe here once the on-brand drafting loop is proven.
content.fuelIntro: Fuel (Emily Kramer, MKT1): the assets that power distribution. Every piece ladders to a messaging pillar, a segment, and the goal (first paying customers). The product's own output is the cheapest, most credible fuel we have.

engines.name: Founder-led LinkedIn
engines.note: Deniz + Won posts drove the discovery campaign. Best fit for this buyer. Carries POV + research + build-in-public.

engines.name: Meta / Instagram (paid + organic)
engines.note: Works for Kids and Swim. Carries product proof: before/after, shot-in-minutes, per-segment demos.

engines.name: SEO / owned
engines.note: The research report, the cost calculator, and the comparison posts are linkable and rank over time.

engines.name: Lifecycle email (Klaviyo)
engines.note: Carries proof and the AHA nudge to signups. Wire the prepared flows first.

fuel.title: The State of On-Model Imagery 2026
fuel.format: Data report + landing page + LinkedIn carousel
fuel.why: Original data is the most linkable, credible fuel. Survey small fashion brands: shoot spend, lead times, AI adoption %, the hardest body to cast. Becomes the flagship asset everything else cites. Seed it from a poll of the signup list.

fuel.title: The photoshoot is becoming optional
fuel.why: Names the shift and the wedge (the bodies you could never afford to shoot). Founder voice is the proven engine for this buyer.

fuel.title: I shot an entire kids' drop in 20 minutes
fuel.pillar: P1 campaign-ready in minutes + P3 ship without a shoot
fuel.why: The lead segment's exact pain: you cannot cheaply shoot babies. Shows the result first.

fuel.title: Every body, every size, no casting
fuel.pillar: P1 bodies you can't shoot + P4 not AI slop
fuel.format: One product on many bodies, video
fuel.why: The most acute, most expensive shoot in fashion. Lead with garment fidelity to pre-empt the AI-slop objection.

fuel.title: AI on-model vs the $5K photoshoot: a real cost breakdown
fuel.why: Honest cost + time comparison, including where real shoots still win. Pairs with the calculator (F9).

fuel.title: Background removal is not on-model: where free tools stop
fuel.why: Reframes against the ~$5 free-tool floor (the PhotoRoom lesson). Teaches the market that the defensible job is the believable on-model shot.

fuel.title: On-model in minutes: live teardown series
fuel.why: Pick a real small brand's product page, show the AI on-model result. Endless, cheap, repeatable fuel straight from product output.

fuel.title: How [Water Vixen Swim / Agenzia Kids] ships a drop without a photographer
fuel.pillar: P1 + P5 built by fashion people
fuel.why: The proof we most need to earn: a named, permissioned Studio customer. Blocked until a brand says yes.

fuel.title: Photoshoot cost calculator
fuel.why: Input drops, SKUs, models, get estimated shoot cost vs Ablo. Lead-gen + linkable + a reason to share.

fuel.title: Build-in-public: what the data taught us about small fashion brands
fuel.why: Share what the OS surfaces (the bodies brands can't shoot, the funnel learnings). Builds the credibility that we are fashion people, not a generic image-AI toy.

content.calendarNote: The Content Calendar tab schedules from this fuel backlog. Near-term pieces are seeded there; the backlog is the source of ideas.

competition.intro: Studio is positioned against budget categories, not feature-comparison competitors.

set.name: The photoshoot & budget
set.what: Editorial agencies, photographers, studios, model agencies. The full $5K to $50K shoot.
set.pitch: No photographers. No studios. No hassle. Replace the shoot budget, not augment it. Speed and refresh frequency are the wedge.
set.win: Hero campaigns, physical garment quality, or licensing concerns that demand real models.

set.name: Horizontal / generic image AI
set.what: Midjourney, PhotoRoom, Pimeli, ChatGPT image, Flux. General tools fashion brands hack into a try-on workflow.
set.pitch: Studio is a workflow, not a model. Fashion-specific: garment fidelity, fabric texture, body proportions, 2K on-model output, multiple poses, URL product import. Free tools own background removal; do not wedge there.
set.win: Creative latitude beyond fashion, or a team comfortable wrangling prompts. PhotoRoom wins the commoditized flat-lay / background job at about $5/mo.

set.name: Vertical AI try-on
set.what: Raspberry AI, Botika, Lalaland, Fashn, Vmodel. Direct feature competitors in AI virtual try-on.
set.pitch: Aware of, not currently messaged against. Raspberry's $50/mo tier is the internal pricing benchmark (the reason Studio's entry came down), not a messaging target.
set.win: As the category matures, these become the named comparison. Today they are an internal benchmark.

differentiation: A fashion-specific workflow (model gen + product import + try-on + edit), not a one-shot tool.
Cafe24 and Shopify import baked in.
Parent-brand credibility lends enterprise trust, even without public Studio reference customers yet.

battlecard.intro: Head-to-head against the AI try-on set. For each competitor: their angle, their soft spots, the objection a buyer will raise, and how Studio wins the room. Studio is the column everything is measured against.
battlecard.caveat: Cells marked VERIFY are unconfirmed and need a check before going in any external deck. Pricing sourced from public pages on 2026-04-28/29; some competitors wall prices behind login. Refresh this card when the workbook updates.

competitors.name: Botika
competitors.tagline: "AI Fashion Model Generator. Turn Flat Lays into On-Model Photos."
strengths: Shopify-native distribution + volume
Affordable entry ($22/mo) + 4-tier ladder
Public reviews exist (mixed but visible) → buyer can find them
weaknesses: 2.7/5 Shopify rating, quality + cancellation complaints
AI distortion patterns (textures, fingers, logos) per industry analysis
No enterprise positioning, capped TAM
wins: Quality the creative director will sign off on. Botika reviews show distorted output; show 5 of our outputs against theirs.
Direct URL import from any e-com site, not just Shopify. Cafe24-native is uncontested.
We're the editorial-quality option. Botika is the cheap-and-fast option. Different jobs.

competitors.name: Raspberry AI
competitors.icp: In-house fashion designers, brand design teams
competitors.differentiation: a16z capital + designer ICP + premium-only motion
strengths: a16z capital + designer-cool brand
Premium-only motion = high ARPU + low support burden
Tight ICP (designers) + sketch-to-render is genuinely differentiated
weaknesses: No free trial + demo-gated funnel limits SMB discovery
Not direct competition for e-com on-model imagery
$298/mo entry = no SMB on-ramp
competitors.objection: 'We use Raspberry for sketch-to-render, why switch?'
wins: Different buyer. We sell to e-com + creative; Raspberry sells to in-house designers.
We're 2K standard with reference-photo lock. Designer can iterate the SAME model across the whole drop.
Premium pricing without demo gate. Self-serve free trial = lower BD friction.

competitors.name: Browzwear (Lalaland)
competitors.tagline: "Custom AI Models for Product Approval, B2B & E-Commerce."
competitors.promise: AI models inside the 3D design + wholesale pipeline
competitors.icp: Mid-large fashion + wholesale brands using Browzwear 3D
competitors.differentiation: Sustainability + 3D-design pipeline integration + 6yrs Lalaland diversity legacy
competitors.customers: None visible (Lalaland legacy: Tommy Hilfiger, Calvin Klein, Levi's)
strengths: VStitcher install base + 3D pipeline integration
Sustainability angle (no physical samples) + size-inclusive legacy
Wholesale workflows + 6yr Lalaland diversity messaging
weaknesses: Lalaland is no longer standalone, buyers w/o Browzwear face heavy integration
No clear e-commerce-first positioning (design-centric)
Enterprise pricing opaque, no SMB tier yet
competitors.objection: 'We already use Browzwear/VStitcher, Lalaland comes with it'
wins: Lalaland is now a feature inside a 3D-design suite. We're a productized try-on workflow that lives where your products already live (Cafe24, Shopify).
We don't require buying a 3D design suite. Standalone, productized, ships in days.
Korean SMBs (Cafe24), uncontested by Browzwear which doesn't surface this market.

competitors.name: The New Black
competitors.differentiation: All-in-one creative studio (design → try-on → video → marketing)
competitors.perImage: $0.125/credit (Lagerfeld); per-image cost depends on credit consumption [VERIFY]
strengths: All-in-one creative studio (design + try-on + video)
Designer ICP loyalty + 500k user claim + video included
Free tier + transparent credit-pack pricing (one-time)
weaknesses: Pricing transparency very low (credit math opaque)
No video specs, no integrations, no Shopify
No named brands, no API, weak integrations
competitors.objection: 'TNB is one tool for design + try-on + video, you only do try-on'
wins: We're built for fashion brands, not designers. Garment fidelity, not sketch-to-render.
Reference-photo model lock + URL import beat their workflow at the on-model imagery step.
We're enterprise-ready (parent's Crocs/Balmain track record). TNB's SMB-only motion can't pitch enterprise.

competitors.name: Fashn.ai
competitors.icp: Devs/enterprise via API; SMBs via web app
competitors.differentiation: Dual web/API + commercial license + 'pre-trained on millions'
strengths: Cheapest API in the set ($0.075/img) + commercial license
Dual web + API serves both creators and enterprise devs
fal.ai distribution + 'pre-trained on millions' technical pitch
weaknesses: Trustpilot complaints: 'no cancel button', poor support
'Daily limits' on Pro/Agency vs monthly credits = harder to plan
Limited public reviews (3 on Trustpilot) = unproven
wins: We're not in the API race. We're in the 'embedded inside your brand pipeline' race. Different sale.
'Cancel anytime' verbatim on our pricing. Their reviews say no cancel button. Trust angle.
Editorial brand voice and fashion-tech category fit. Fashn looks and reads like a developer tool.

competitors.name: Veeton
competitors.promise: Photoreal catalog at 3x lower cost, 2x conversion
competitors.icp: E-com SMB to mid-market, creative studios
competitors.differentiation: Pairing & styling + batch consistency + named EU customers
competitors.reviews: HEC case study, no aggregate rating
competitors.customers: Carrefour, Fjörk Merino, Inès de la Fressange
strengths: Named premium customers (Carrefour, Inès de la Fressange)
Explicit batch mode + consistency across catalogs
EU/HEC case study credibility + multi-language reach
weaknesses: No public free trial / pricing opacity at Pro tier

funnelCurated.note: Distinct users reaching each milestone, ordered along the happy path. Reach (not strict order), so a later step can exceed an earlier one when users take a side path (try-on via Surprise Me without importing, checkout from pricing without downloading). The activation spine below is the same-user, strictly monotonic view.

spine.label: Activation spine
spine.note: Same user, of everyone who signed up (since launch). Strictly monotonic, this is the cleanest drop story.

drops.title: Engagement gap (land → engage)
drops.detail: Only 161 of 752 visitors click any CTA. The hero is not converting the visit into intent.
drops.fix: Sharpen the hero promise and CTA; lead from the bodies-you-cannot-shoot wedge.

drops.title: Signup-modal leak (open → signup)
drops.detail: 148 open the signup modal, only 64 finish. Email magic-link leaked almost everyone (retired); the in-modal drop is the standing lever.
drops.fix: Google one-click as the primary, full-width button; demote email. Ship mobile-first (71% of opens).

drops.title: Activation gap (signup → model)
drops.detail: 1 in 3 signups never generate a model. The largest rage-click cluster by far sits on /studio (10 users, 30 clicks).
drops.fix: Find the control that looks clickable but stalls before the first Generate. Coachmarks experiment is live.

drops.title: Value-capture leak (try-on → download)
drops.detail: Only 5 of 30 users who reach the aha export a result. The aha fires but the value never leaves the app.
drops.fix: Add a post-try-on download / share prompt the moment the image renders.

drops.title: Payment + measurement (try-on → checkout)
drops.detail: 7 of 30 aha-reachers start checkout, and 0 purchases are confirmed because no success event fires.
drops.fix: In-product upgrade prompt at the aha, plus the price-ask test. Instrument purchase_completed.

gaps: No purchase_completed / subscription_started event is instrumented. checkout_started (8 users) is the deepest tracked step, so true paid conversion and revenue cannot be read from product analytics yet. Instrument it to close the loop.
studio_entered (80) exceeds signup_completed (64) because the studio is reachable by returning and anonymous sessions. The activation spine corrects for this by counting same-user, signup-anchored.

lifecycleCurated.note: Klaviyo already receives the product events as metrics (Model Generated, Try-on Completed, Checkout Started), so behavioral triggers are possible today. Only the Added-to-List onboarding flow is live; the behavioral flows that would fix the funnel are unbuilt even though the emails exist.

liveFlows.read: Elite open rates (96-100%) but near-zero clicks (2.5%). The emails get seen and do not pull anyone back into the product. Add one clear, single CTA per email tied to the next funnel step.

prepared.name: [Ablo Lifecycle] Activate A2 — kill the blank page
[Ablo Lifecycle] AHA B1 — the missing half
[Ablo Lifecycle] AHA B2 — show the outcome
[Ablo Lifecycle] AHA B3 — last nudge + offer help

draftFlows.name: Welcome Series - Standard
draftFlows.note: Ablo-relevant, built Apr 2, never turned on.

opportunities.title: Activate the 'generated a model, never tried on' segment
opportunities.use: Wire the AHA B1-B3 templates into a 3-step flow.
opportunities.impact: Targets the model → try-on drop. The emails already exist.

opportunities.title: Rescue the 'signed up, never generated' segment
opportunities.trigger: Added to List, no Model Generated in 24h
opportunities.impact: Targets the 33% activation gap, the biggest post-signup leak.

opportunities.title: Convert the 'tried on, never checked out' segment
opportunities.use: New value / upgrade email at the aha moment.
opportunities.impact: Targets the 77% who reach the aha but never start payment.

channels.intro: Every acquisition and retention surface in one scorecard, so budget concentration is a decision, not a guess. The open question the OS exists to answer: which one channel plus audience to pour budget into.

rows.name: Meta / Instagram Ads
rows.read: Flight ended May 29 and delivery broke May 30-31 (billing). Kids is the cheapest, healthiest segment; Swim has the best CTR (2.97%). Run by the ads autopilot on a 6h cron.

rows.name: LinkedIn (founder-led)
rows.read: Wave 1 (Deniz 5/19, Won 5/20) plus ~$300 Thought Leader Ads. US decisively outperforms EU. No standing cadence yet.

rows.name: Email / Klaviyo
rows.read: One onboarding flow live. 6 lifecycle emails built but wired to nothing. The cheapest untapped lever in the stack.

rows.name: Organic / Content
rows.read: No content engine by design. The product's own output (before/after, shot-in-minutes) is the latent, infinite fuel when content goes in scope.

channels.decision: Evidence points to Meta + Kids/Swim as the paid wedge to concentrate on once delivery is restored, with email as the cheapest immediate lever (the activation and aha flows are already built, just unwired). LinkedIn stays founder-led and US-only. Organic is deferred.

commandCenter.intro: The prioritized action queue, anchored to the goal. Every live funnel leak is tied to the one fix that moves it and to the KPI it ladders up to, so priority always means goal-impact. This is the surface the daily routine rewrites as it reads the funnel, campaigns, experiments and lifecycle and learns which fixes moved which number.

items.title: Wire the prepared lifecycle emails
items.body: The Activate (A1-A3) and AHA (B1-B3) templates exist but sit in no flow. Build two behavioral flows in Klaviyo (Added-to-List → Activate, Model Generated → AHA). Zero new copy, ships this week.

items.title: Fix the signup-modal leak
items.body: Promote Google one-click to the primary, full-width button and demote email. Mobile-first. Tracked by the signup experiment.

items.title: Close the activation gap
items.body: Pull /studio session replays on the non-activators and find the control that stalls before the first Generate. Rage-click cluster confirmed. Coachmarks experiment live.

items.title: Stop the value-capture leak
items.body: Only 5 of 30 aha-reachers export a result. Add a download / share prompt the instant the try-on renders, so the value leaves the app.

items.title: Instrument purchase_completed
items.body: No paid-conversion event fires, so true signup→paid and revenue cannot be measured. This blocks the make-or-break number the whole strategy hinges on.

items.title: Restart Meta delivery, concentrate Kids/Swim
items.body: Fix billing in Ads Manager, then let the autopilot reconcentrate spend on the Kids and Swim winners. Two zero-spend days flagged.

items.title: Run the price-ask test
items.body: Manually onboard 5 kids and 5 swim founders, watch them hit try-on, then ask what they would pay. Free signups tell us almost nothing about revenue.
items.ladder: The brag · ARPU ≥ $50, validate willingness to pay

commandCenter.loop: The daily routine: read the live funnel, campaigns, experiments and lifecycle, re-rank this queue by impact on the KPIs above, update the status of every in-flight item, sync against ClickUp (the task source of truth), and write the result back. Over time it correlates each shipped fix with the KPI it moved, so the ranking gets smarter on its own.

playbook.title: Orient
playbook.detail: Pull all live data, re-read the goal (first paying customer, CAC < $300, signup→paid ≥ 8%). Everything ranks against it.

playbook.title: Experiments
playbook.detail: Check every experiment by ID, report how it trends, conclude the ones with enough sample, queue the follow-up. Do not call a winner under-powered.

playbook.title: Funnel
playbook.detail: Find the biggest leak by leverage. Correlate each shipped fix to the number it moved. Write concrete improvement hypotheses.

playbook.title: Campaigns
playbook.detail: Read the Meta autopilot. Extract the key insight, flag waste and anomalies (zero-spend days, CPL spikes, budget off the converting segments).

playbook.title: Lifecycle
playbook.detail: Check Klaviyo flows. Name the highest-leverage email gap (a prepared flow wired to nothing).

playbook.title: Integrity
playbook.detail: Confirm the key events still fire (signup, model, try-on, purchase_completed). A silently broken metric corrupts every conclusion.

playbook.title: Re-rank
playbook.detail: Rewrite this queue by leverage. Resolve done items, update statuses with the delta, add new leaks as items.

playbook.title: Build the next test
playbook.detail: Design the next experiment from the top leak (hypothesis, variant, metric, audience). Draft it in PostHog, or spec it if write access is missing.

playbook.title: Publish + report
playbook.detail: Commit and push, log what moved and concluded, summarize in 3 lines. Escalate only money, pricing, access, or user-visible product calls.

objectives.northStar: First paying customer plus a repeatable channel at stable CAC.
objectives.rule: Every item below earns its place by moving one of these. The agent ranks by goal-impact: anything that does not ladder up to a KPI does not belong in the queue.

contentCalendar.note: Seeded scaffold. The source of truth will be ClickUp — a reminder task is queued to wire this to a ClickUp calendar. Until then, edit items here. Instagram publishing by the agent needs an IG token refresh (instagram_content_publish scope).

pricing.note: Verify live pricing on the site before putting a number in copy. A $50/mo SMB tier was decided 2026-04-27; the old $1,250/mo Pro entry was being removed ("Option B" shipped on site 2026-05-04). Do not write to the old $1,250 price point.

rows.v: Model gen 5 (10 with reference photo) · Try-on 5 · Edit 2 · Import free

gtm.intro: The bridge between goals and tactics, built on Emily Kramer's MKT1 framework. Goals say where we are going. This says how we win. The execution tabs (campaigns, experiments, content, lifecycle) are the how-we-do-it.
gtm.motion: Self-serve, product-led, wedge-first. Founder-led LinkedIn plus paid social seed a fast, visual free trial for a tightly-defined buyer (founders of small US fashion brands), led by the one job free tools cannot do: the believable on-model shot of bodies you cannot cheaply photograph. The enterprise / embedded motion runs separately, owned by Michael, and is out of scope here.

stack.what: First paying customers, CAC < $300, signup→paid ≥ 8%. North-star: liquid revenue via self-serve.
The wedge (who + the one job), the marketing advantages we exploit, the perceptions we drive, and the 1 to 3 Big Bets that combine them. This tab.
The campaigns, experiments, content fuel, and lifecycle flows that execute the Big Bets. Ranked daily in the Command Center.
stack.where: Campaigns · Experiments · Content · Lifecycle · Command Center

gtm.wedge: Wedge marketing (MKT1): win a specific niche before expanding. Lead with Kids & babywear (the cheapest, healthiest funnel, the hardest body to shoot), co-lead Swim & size-inclusive (best attention, most acute pain). Tailor the creative, demo, and landing to the wedge. Do not market the pitch deck; market one niche first. Full tiers in ICP & Segments.

bigBets.name: Win the wedge (Kids + Swim)
bigBets.icp: Founders of small US Kids & babywear (lead) + Swim / size-inclusive (co-lead) brands
bigBets.perception: P1, campaign-ready on-model photos in minutes, including the bodies I can't shoot
bigBets.fuel: Before/after, shot-in-minutes, per-segment demos (F3, F4, F7)
bigBets.success: Stable-CAC signups and activations from Kids/Swim that feed the price-ask test

bigBets.name: Prove willingness to pay
bigBets.perception: P3, I can ship a drop without a photographer, studio, or casting
bigBets.fuel: AHA nudge flow + the price-ask interview guide
bigBets.success: First 5 paying customers, ARPU ≥ $50, signup→paid ≥ 8% (the brag)

bigBets.name: Build the credibility flywheel
bigBets.icp: The broad small-fashion-founder market (+ enterprise halo)
bigBets.perception: P2 the photoshoot is becoming optional, + P5 built by fashion people
bigBets.advantage: Fuel advantage (unique POV + data) + founder distribution
bigBets.fuel: The State of On-Model Imagery report (F1), comparison (F6), build-in-public (F10), calculator (F9)
bigBets.success: A linkable, cited asset that drives inbound and lowers CAC over time

gtm.channelNote: Channel strategy uses MKT1's five growth engines (Inbound, Outbound, Product Virality, Events, Ecosystem). For Ablo today: Founder LinkedIn is the primary engine, Meta/IG is the demand engine, SEO/owned is the compounding bet, lifecycle is activation. Full scorecard and status in the Channels tab.
gtm.frameworkNote: Method: MKT1's seven strategy exercises (company overview, ICP prioritization, marketing advantages, perceptions, positioning, revenue levers, big bets). The first six live across Overview, ICP, Positioning, Messaging, and Goals. This tab is exercise 7, the Big Bets, where strategy becomes coordinated action.

agents.intro: Read this first. This page orients an AI agent to the Marketing OS before it acts. The OS is built to be pinged and used by agents continuously.
agents.what: The Marketing OS is the single context layer and task list for Ablo Studio's marketing. One source of truth for strategy (positioning, ICP, messaging, voice, GTM) and live execution (funnel, experiments, campaigns, lifecycle). If you are doing any Ablo Studio marketing work, ground yourself here first.
agents.goal: The end state is an autonomous, self-improving marketing system that manages every aspect of marketing. Near-term north-star: first paying customers at CAC < $300 and signup→paid ≥ 8%. Everything you do ranks against that goal.

howToUse.v: Read Overview (what Ablo is), Goals & OKRs (where we are going), and GTM Strategy (how we win). Those three ground almost any task.
The Command Center is the ranked action queue. The daily playbook (the marketing-os-refresh skill) is the operating loop: check experiments, funnel, campaigns, lifecycle, content, then re-rank.

Funnel, Experiments, Campaigns, and Lifecycle are LIVE (PostHog, Meta, Klaviyo), refreshed on every build. Positioning, ICP, Messaging, Voice, and GTM are curated and human-edited. Never auto-rewrite curated strategy.

Lead every message from the wedge (the bodies brands cannot shoot). Obey the Brand Voice rules (no em dashes, no hype, editorial, lead with the result). Use the copy-paste voice card on the Brand Voice tab.
Every content piece must ladder to a messaging pillar, a segment, and the goal. Pull ideas from the Content fuel backlog and schedule on the Content Calendar.
Run no more than 2 to 3 at once. Refer to each by its ID. Do not conclude under-powered tests.
Escalate only money/budget, pricing/positioning, access/credentials, and user-visible product changes. Everything else (analysis, re-ranking, drafting, instrumentation, wiring a flow) you own.
The OS rebuilds daily. Each tab shows an updated stamp. If a live source failed, the curated fallback is shown and labelled.

canonical.v: Ablo Studio, self-serve AI on-model imagery for fashion brands. Create a model, paste a product URL, get campaign-ready 2K imagery in minutes.
Founder / owner of a small (2 to 50, mostly 2 to 10) US fashion brand. Wedge: Kids & babywear (lead), Swim / size-inclusive (co-lead).
The believable on-model shot of a body the brand cannot easily or cheaply photograph. Not background removal, free tools own that.
Pre-revenue. End-of-June goal: first paying customer.

agents.consume: For structured data, read data.js (window.ABLO_OS) at the site root: every section is a key, live data sits under .live. The Brand Voice tab carries a copy-paste voice card built for content agents.
